name: Good Grief & Co.
philosophy: Death deserves better than beige carpet and organ music. We help people plan their own funerals, living wakes, and death parties while they're still alive and opinionated about the playlist. Equal parts tender and irreverent, we believe grief is not the absence of joy — it's the proof that joy was there.

logo.clearSpace: Minimum clear space equal to the height of the ampersand in the logo
donts: Never place the logo on a busy photographic background without a backing shape
Never render the logo in colors outside the brand palette
Never distort or rotate the logo

primary.name: Amethyst
primary.role: Primary brand — hero backgrounds, headings, key UI elements

primary.name: Matte Black
primary.role: Foundation — body text, dark sections, nav backgrounds

secondary.name: Hot Magenta
secondary.role: Pop — hover states, callouts, emphasis moments

web.name: Soft Lilac
web.role: Light accent — cards, hover tints, borders

heading.usage: Page titles, hero headlines, section headings. Condensed serif-like impact. Always uppercase for H1, mixed case for H2–H4.

subheading.usage: Section subtitles, card titles, pull quotes

body.usage: Body copy, descriptions, form labels. Humanist warmth balances the editorial headlines.

eyebrow.usage: Category labels, section identifiers, meta info. Letterspaced for editorial feel.

button.usage: CTAs and navigation links. Uppercase, slightly letterspaced.

rules: Headlines use Oswald Bold uppercase for maximum editorial impact
Body text uses Source Sans 3 Regular at 18px/1.6 for readability
Never use more than two font sizes in a single content block
Pull quotes use Oswald Medium italic when available

photography.style: Warm, saturated, intimate. Shot in the converted shotgun house — velvet textures, neon glow, candlelight. People laughing, toasting, crying happy tears. Polaroid wall aesthetic. Golden hour and moody interior lighting.
rules: Always warm color grade — no cool/clinical tones
Favor candid moments over posed portraits
Include environmental details: velvet furniture, neon signs, cocktails, flowers
Depth of field should feel intimate — shallow focus on subjects, bokeh on neon/candles
Diversity in subjects: age, ethnicity, body type, relationship configuration
donts: No stark white or clinical lighting
No traditional funeral imagery — no caskets, hearses, or black veils
No stock-photo-perfect people in sterile environments
No desaturated or muted color palettes
photography.social: Behind-the-scenes Polaroid style. Slightly imperfect framing. Warm flash photography at events. User-generated content encouraged.

voice.character: Your wise, witty friend who brings champagne to the hard conversations. Warm but never saccharine. Direct but never cold. We talk about death the way we talk about life — with honesty, humor, and a cocktail in hand.

do.text: Plan your party. Yes, that one.
do.context: Concise, knowing, inviting — doesn't shy from the subject

do.text: You have opinions about your funeral playlist. We're here for it.
do.context: Meets people where they are with humor and warmth

do.text: Grief is just love with nowhere to go. Let's give it a dance floor.
do.context: Poetic but grounded — elevates without being precious

do.text: We've helped 200+ people throw the party of a lifetime. Their lifetime.
do.context: Confident, slightly cheeky, backed by credibility

dont.text: We provide end-of-life celebration facilitation services.
dont.context: Corporate jargon strips all warmth and personality
Too flippant — trivializes the emotional weight

dont.text: In this difficult time, we are here for you.
dont.context: Generic sympathy card language — exactly what we're replacing

dont.text: YOLO so plan your funeral NOW!
dont.context: Crass urgency — disrespects the gravity of the decision

rules: Never use the word 'passed' — say 'died' or 'dead' with respect
Never use funeral industry euphemisms ('remains', 'the deceased', 'interment')
Humor should punch up, never down
Always center the person, not the service
It's okay to be funny. It's not okay to be flippant.

tone.description: Tone shifts based on context: celebratory and bold on marketing pages, warm and steady during planning conversations, tender and present in grief support. The irreverence dials up for awareness content and dials down for active grief.

writingGoals.description: Make death planning feel as natural as wedding planning. Remove stigma through casual confidence.
Frame end-of-life planning as an act of love and self-expression, not morbid obligation.
Give people agency over their own story. Your life, your party, your rules.

cleverVsClear.clever: Headlines, taglines, social media, awareness campaigns — this is where personality lives loudest
cleverVsClear.clear: Service descriptions, pricing, planning guides, grief resources — clarity is kindness here

contentPillars.name: Living Wakes
contentPillars.description: Celebrations held while the guest of honor is still alive to enjoy them. The flagship experience.

contentPillars.name: Death Party Planning
contentPillars.description: Helping people design their own funeral or memorial — music, food, dress code, readings, everything.

contentPillars.name: Grief Support
contentPillars.description: Post-loss support that doesn't look like a support group. Cocktail hours, storytelling nights, creative workshops.

contentPillars.name: Death Positivity
contentPillars.description: Education and advocacy content normalizing conversations about mortality, end-of-life wishes, and death culture.

personas.name: The Life-of-the-Party Planner
personas.description: 50–70, knows exactly what songs they want played and has strong opinions about flower arrangements. Wants their send-off to feel like them, not like a template.
personas.motto: If I have to go, I'm going out on my terms.

stats.description: Have thought about their funeral but never discussed it
More likely to plan if framed as celebration vs. obligation

personas.name: The Loving Organizer
personas.description: 35–55, planning for an aging parent or processing a recent loss. Wants guidance, not platitudes. Appreciates the irreverence because it cuts through the heaviness.
personas.motto: I want to do this right — and 'right' shouldn't mean 'sad.'

stats.description: Say traditional funeral services felt impersonal
Average time spent on site before booking a consultation
